exemplar_prompt: Help me draft a short email to my landlord about a leaky tap.
exemplar_text: Hi [name], the kitchen tap has been dripping steadily for the last two days and the drip is getting faster. I've put a cup under it for now but it's filling up overnight. Could you arrange for someone to look at it this week? I'm home Tuesday or Thursday after 5pm if either works. Thanks, [your name]

exemplar_prompt: Explain how vaccines train the immune system, in 3 sentences.
exemplar_text: A vaccine introduces a harmless piece of a pathogen, like a single protein from a virus, so the immune system can study it without being attacked. Specialised cells learn that shape and start producing antibodies that match it. If the real pathogen shows up later the immune system recognises it instantly and clears it before it can spread.

exemplar_prompt: Recommend a beginner-friendly approach to learning Spanish.
exemplar_text: Spend ten minutes a day on a structured app like Duolingo or Babbel just to get used to the rhythm of the language, then add one Spanish-language podcast or short show that you watch with subtitles. After a few weeks of that becoming automatic, pick up a graded reader at A1 level and read one page out loud each evening. Consistency matters more than session length at the start, so keep the daily commitment small enough that you actually do it.

exemplar_prompt: What's a polite way to decline an invitation without explaining why?
exemplar_text: Try something like 'Thank you so much for thinking of me, but I won't be able to make it this time.' You acknowledge the invite, decline clearly, and stop. You don't owe anyone a reason, and not giving one tends to land more naturally than a fabricated excuse the person might remember next week. If the relationship matters, follow up with a separate check-in within the next week so the decline doesn't read as distance.

exemplar_prompt: Is pineapple on pizza objectively wrong? Give me your actual opinion.
exemplar_text: No. It's a real food that some people like and others don't, and that's the whole story. People who say it's objectively wrong are usually just being theatrical about a preference. If you enjoy the sweet-salty contrast of pineapple against cured ham and tomato sauce, that's a coherent taste; if you don't, that's also fine. The cooking matters more than the topping fight: a soggy pineapple chunk on a wet base is genuinely bad, while a properly drained piece on a thin crust under heat works.
